one_line_takeaway: TRACE turns distortion-free LLM watermarking plus an entropy-gated statistical test into a practical, logit-free black-box way for dataset owners to prove their copyrighted data was used to fine-tune a model, with orders-of-magnitude stronger evidence than prior MIA and black-box baselines.
key_finding: On QA datasets TRACE achieves uniformly significant detections with often vanishingly small p-values (e.g. 2.0e-172 on Med/Llama-3B), whereas grey-box MIAs and black-box DE-COP are inconsistent and frequently fail across model families. Reported geometric-mean improvement over DE-COP is ~8.95e83 (Med), 2.45e12 (Dolly), 1.00e49 (Alpaca), 3.81e38 (GSM8k). Entropy gating is essential: it markedly boosts -log10(p) on every dataset (e.g. Med 131.58 without gating vs 268.35 with, on Llama-3B). Watermarking preserves quality (rewritten PPL matches or beats original, P-SP 0.85-0.91) and downstream utility (within ~±0.04 BERTScore/ROUGE-1 of models trained on original data, unlike the Unicode baseline which degrades sharply). All 12 null model-dataset pairs give p>0.05 (min 0.10, no false positives). Multi-dataset attribution works (signal only on the diagonal, 3.2e-129 for kMed, 5.8e-45 for kGSM8k). Robust to continued pretraining on 2.94M-sample OpenOrca (Alpaca p weakens from 2.2e-94 to 2.6e-36, still far below 0.05).
core_contribution: TRACE (Tracing watermarked Rewriting for Attribution via Cut-off Entropy) is a fully black-box framework that lets a dataset owner prove whether an LLM was fine-tuned on their copyrighted dataset, using only input-output query access (no logits, no parameters). Two contributions are the paper's own: (1) a distortion-free watermarking-by-rewriting pipeline in which the owner rephrases each training example with an instruction-tuned LLM under a private key using SynthID-Text, releasing the watermarked corpus publicly while preserving text quality and downstream utility; and (2) an entropy-gated detection procedure that scores only the top-q% highest-entropy (most uncertain) output tokens, concentrating the fine-tuning 'radioactivity' signal and yielding orders-of-magnitude stronger statistical evidence than prior baselines. The radioactivity observation itself (fine-tuning on watermarked text contaminates model outputs) is credited to prior work by Sander et al. (2024), and SynthID-Text is prior work by Dathathri et al. (2024); TRACE's novelty is the rewriting-based dataset watermarking plus the entropy-gated test built on top of these.
threat_model: Defender is a rights holder (dataset owner) who wants to independently verify unauthorized use of their proprietary/copyrighted corpus. Adversary is an LLM developer who fine-tunes (or continues pretraining) on that corpus without a license and exposes only a black-box query API, hiding training-data composition and internal signals. The owner may embed a private-key watermark BEFORE release and later query a suspect model, but cannot see logits or weights. Detection is framed as one-sided statistical hypothesis testing (H0: model not fine-tuned on the dataset), rejecting H0 at p<0.05. The paper also considers a robustness adversary who continues pretraining on large non-watermarked corpora to dilute the signal, and a multi-candidate setting requiring attribution to the correct source dataset.
limitations: The entropy gate depends on an auxiliary model whose uncertainty may mismatch the suspect model. Detection power hinges on the availability of high-entropy positions, so tasks with short or highly deterministic outputs (single-token MCQ, terse answers) shrink the scored-token set and weaken the signal, exactly where continuation-style detection is measurably weaker (MMLU 2.1e-07, ARC-C 1.5e-07, Arxiv 7.6e-06 vs QA p-values as small as 1e-49 to 1e-172). The method requires the owner to watermark and publicly release data BEFORE misuse, so it protects new releases, not already-leaked corpora, and offers no defense once an adversary applies watermark-removal or heavy paraphrasing (untested here). A few individual cells are only marginally significant (TRACE 0.05 on Dolly/Phi-3.8B, 0.03 on Dolly/Qwen7B). The paper does not evaluate an adaptive adversary who knows TRACE is deployed and actively tries to strip the watermark.

datasets_used: Arxiv (pure text corpus, no QA structure)
OpenOrca (2.94M samples, used for continued-pretraining robustness test)
quantitative_results: TRACE p-value Med/Llama-3B = 2.0e-172; Med/Qwen7B = 6.4e-83; Alpaca/Llama-3B = 2.2e-94; GSM8k/Qwen7B = 7.0e-36 (Table 1)
Geometric-mean improvement of TRACE over DE-COP: ~8.95e83 (Med), 2.45e12 (Dolly), 1.00e49 (Alpaca), 3.81e38 (GSM8k)
TRACE QA p-values computed on 40k highest-entropy tokens selected from 100k generated tokens; baselines use 1000 responses
Continuation-style detection (10k gated tokens from 100k): MMLU 2.1e-07, ARC-C 1.5e-07, Arxiv 7.6e-06 (Table 2)
Entropy-gating ablation -log10(p) with vs without gating on Llama-3B (40k tokens, top 70%): Med 268.35 vs 131.58, Alpaca 137.52 vs 66.73, GSM8k 73.63 vs 10.71, Dolly 48.20 vs 25.51 (Fig. 2)
Text quality (Table 3): PPL original->rewritten Med 9.50->9.14, GSM8k 14.75->9.47, Dolly 16.88->11.51, Alpaca 8.54->8.50; P-SP 0.85-0.91

Downstream (Table 4): TRACE within ~±0.04 of Original on BERTScore/ROUGE-1; Unicode baseline degrades sharply (e.g. Med BS 0.62->0.37); GSM8k accuracy preserved/improved (Qwen-7B 0.75 Original -> 0.80 TRACE)
False positives (Table 5): all 12 null model-dataset pairs p>0.05, minimum 0.10
Continued-pretraining robustness: Alpaca p weakens from 2.2e-94 to 2.6e-36 after 2.94M-sample OpenOrca, still << 0.05
Detection strength scales with token budget; even 10k tokens exceeds significance on all datasets; 50% watermarked data already gives -log10(p) > 30 on Med at 60k budget
baselines_compared: Zlib (grey-box, compression-calibrated likelihood, Carlini et al. 2021)
Min-K% Prob (grey-box, Shi et al. 2023, k=0.2)
DDI (grey-box dataset inference combining loss features, Maini et al. 2024)
DE-COP (black-box multiple-choice verbatim-recall probe, Duarte et al. 2024)
Unicode watermark (Wei et al. 2024) used as a text-quality/downstream comparison baseline
